EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of strong to severe storms moved southeast across Northwest Kansas.  Hail up to nickel size was reported between Oakley and Grinnell.  The main hazard was the high winds produced by the thunderstorm outflow ahead of the cluster.  The highest measured wind gust was 80 MPH at Oakley.  These high winds snapped a power pole near Angelus on Highway 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms moved across portions of Northwest Kansas during the afternoon.  The largest hail reported was ping-pong ball size near Almena with the first round of storms.  The second round involved a very strong storm moving south out of Nebraska.  Large hail up to baseball size fell for several minutes in the Almena area and near Reager.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to ping-pong ball size has fallen.  Information passed through a spotter.

Two rounds of severe storms occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 8th, 2015. Storms first developed over Southern Kansas and quickly raced into Southeast Kansas during the afternoon. These storms produced 70 to 80 mph winds along with hail up to the size of golf balls. A second round of supercell thunderstorms eventually fired back to the west along a retreating dry line during the early evening over South Central Kansas and Northern Oklahoma. These storms produced several reports of  very large hail and eventually tracked into Kingman county where hail over 3 inches was reported which is larger than the size of baseballs. A brief tornado touchdown occurred southeast of Zenda, Kansas. ||Another supercell thunderstorm formed over eastern Kingman county. Rotating wall clouds were reported near the town of Cheney and a tornado touched down in extreme northwest Sedgwick County and tracked into Southeast Reno county.  The tornado was rated an EF0.

A small compact complex of severe thunderstorms developed near the border of Colorado and Kansas during the late evening hours of April 2nd, 2015.  This severe thunderstorm continued to develop and intensify as it moved east into Central Kansas and took on bow echo-like characteristics. This bow-echo storm later produced quite a punch with damaging straight-line winds and large hail reported across much of Central and South Central Kansas. As the bow-echo approached Sedgwick and Harvey counties, channeled microbursts developed and caused significant straight-line wind damage.  Power was knocked out to over 28,000 residents in the northern sections of Wichita for almost 5 days. Wichita and Maize schools cancelled classes on Friday, April 3rd, due to the power outages.  This dangerous storm continued to move through South Central Kansas during the early morning hours, and took on supercellular characteristics as it moved into Southeast Kansas producing hail up to the size of grapefruits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter size hail across the east side of town, which was piling up like snow.

This dangerous storm continued to move through South Central Kansas during the early morning hours, and took on supercellular characteristics as it moved into Southeast Kansas producing hail up to the size of grapefruits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the evening, a few severe thunderstorms dropped hail across parts of Sedgwick, Sumner, Butler, and Greenwood Counties. Of these counties, by far the largest and most destructive hail occurred in Butler County, where most of El Dorado was pummeled by golf ball to baseball-sized hail.||No doubt, damage was widespread. The most serious occurred along and south of Central Avenue (K-254) from Boyer Road to the Walnut River.||Most vehicles sustained significant damage that no doubt included shattered wind shields. Perhaps the most note worthy was to the John K. Fisher Chevrolet/Cadillac Dealership where around 50 cars were heavily damaged. There was major damage to roofs and siding of countless homes and businesses. The very large hail caused major roof damage on the Butler County Community College campus where most of their vehicles were heavily damaged. Extensive roof damage, that included many broken sky lights, caused the closure of Walmart. Many traffic signals were also damaged and some cases, destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The report was received via Twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another small cluster of storms moved across Central KS and produced a minimal amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured 58 mph wind reported was recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms produced torrential rains across parts of South-Central Kansas for much of the afternoon and evening. Major flooding occurred. The most serious was that of a flash flood that occurred in Wichita. In parts of South and Southeast Wichita as well as West Wichita vehicles were submerged. Most of the parking lots at the Towne West Mall were flooded to depths around 3 feet. Rainfalls from 3-6 inches were widespread. Many streets were barricaded. Among them was Maple Avenue from I-235 to West Streets. The situation was exacerbated by heavy thunderstorms that produced torrential rains early in the morning. In fact, a staggering 5.60 inches was measured in Harper that morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KAKE Channel 10 reported that Maple Avenue is closed from I-235 to West Street. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low spun around the Southwest United States during this time frame with weak upper level shortwaves being ejected eastward across the Central Rockies into the Central High Plains. This caused diffluent westerly flow above the Central High Plains. A strong upper level jet was also in place above the area of concern. Mid-level Isentropic lift of saturated air was present during this event and was the main lifting mechanism. A dome of high pressure shifted eastward as the weekend approached allowing southeast upslope winds to be felt across western Kansas. Light to moderate rain started to fall across far western Kansas by late Saturday morning, spreading eastward as the afternoon progressed. Precipitation then changed over to snow by evening, first across west central Kansas, then the remainder of southwest Kansas by midnight. Moderate snow showers were observed throughout the night with occasional light snow reported throughout Sunday. Light to moderate snow fell once again across portions of western Kansas Sunday night before ending by late Monday morning. By the time the snow ended, snowfall amounts of 6-10 inches were reported across west central Kansas, 3-7 inches across southwest Kansas, and 1-4 inches across central and south central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 6 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 18th a line of thunderstorms rolled through far northern KS. These storms produced rainfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour as they continued to move eastward. Some roads were damaged due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of the township and county roads from 4 to 8 miles east and northeast of Oketo. Some damage to roads as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low spun around the Southwest United States during this time frame with weak upper level shortwaves being ejected eastward across the Central Rockies into the Central High Plains. This caused diffluent westerly flow above the Central High Plains. A strong upper level jet was also in place above the area of concern. Mid-level Isentropic lift of saturated air was present during this event and was the main lifting mechanism. A dome of high pressure shifted eastward as the weekend approached allowing southeast upslope winds to be felt across western Kansas. Light to moderate rain started to fall across far western Kansas by late Saturday morning, spreading eastward as the afternoon progressed. Precipitation then changed over to snow by evening, first across west central Kansas, then the remainder of southwest Kansas by midnight. Moderate snow showers were observed throughout the night with occasional light snow reported throughout Sunday. Light to moderate snow fell once again across portions of western Kansas Sunday night before ending by late Monday morning. By the time the snow ended, snowfall amounts of 6-10 inches were reported across west central Kansas, 3-7 inches across southwest Kansas, and 1-4 inches across central and south central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 8 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed early this Wednesday afternoon and evening, and predominantly produced tornadoes and flash flooding. Five tornadoes occurred over Jewell County. With no cap in place, scattered thunderstorms began developing as early as the noon hour over north central Kansas. Most storms rapidly became supercellular. These supercells began producing severe weather by 330 p.m. CDT. It was about this time that a short line formed from the Jewell-Smith County line south into Osborne County. Supercells were embedded within this line and several discrete supercells developed ahead of it over Mitchell and Jewell counties. Eventually, an MCS developed. The leading edge of the cold pool becoming stationary, with new storms repeatedly developing over north central Kansas and further downstream into southeastern Nebraska. Cell training produced a swath of 4-8 inches of rain over portions of Jewell County. The highest amount reported was 7.92 inches near Webber. ||A complex low pressure system extended from the Canadian Prairies into the western United States. These storms developed in the warm sector, far away from any discernible surface boundaries. However, an axis of confluence was present over north central Kansas. A deep upper-level trough was over the western U.S., with a ridge over the East. In the southwest flow between these features, a shortwave trough was lifting through the Central Plains. These storms formed in the southeast quadrant of the associated vorticity maximum. Around the time thunderstorms developed, temperatures were in the lower 70s with dewpoints in the lower 60s, resulting in low cloud bases. Even at mid-afternoon, cloud bases were only 2000-2500 ft. The surface conditions and mid-level lapse rates of 7 degrees C/km resulted in SBCAPE was around 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was only 25-30 knots. However, low-level wind fields were strong with 40-45 knots at 850 mb. This resulted in large hodographs with 0-1 km SRH values of 200-250 m2/s2, and 0-3 km SRH of 250-300 m2/s2. Combined with low cloud bases, this created a favorable environment for supercells and tornadoes.

These supercells began producing severe weather by 330 p.m. CDT. It was about this time that a short line formed from the Jewell-Smith County line south into Osborne County. Supercells were embedded within this line and several discrete supercells developed ahead of it over Mitchell and Jewell counties. Eventually, an MCS developed. The leading edge of the cold pool becoming stationary, with new storms repeatedly developing over north central Kansas and further downstream into southeastern Nebraska. Cell training produced a swath of 4-8 inches of rain over portions of Jewell County. The highest amount reported was 7.92 inches near Webber. ||A complex low pressure system extended from the Canadian Prairies into the western United States. These storms developed in the warm sector, far away from any discernible surface boundaries. However, an axis of confluence was present over north central Kansas. A deep upper-level trough was over the western U.S., with a ridge over the East. In the southwest flow between these features, a shortwave trough was lifting through the Central Plains. These storms formed in the southeast quadrant of the associated vorticity maximum. Around the time thunderstorms developed, temperatures were in the lower 70s with dewpoints in the lower 60s, resulting in low cloud bases. Even at mid-afternoon, cloud bases were only 2000-2500 ft. The surface conditions and mid-level lapse rates of 7 degrees C/km resulted in SBCAPE was around 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was only 25-30 knots. However, low-level wind fields were strong with 40-45 knots at 850 mb. This resulted in large hodographs with 0-1 km SRH values of 200-250 m2/s2, and 0-3 km SRH of 250-300 m2/s2. Combined with low cloud bases, this created a favorable environment for supercells and tornadoes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous, semi-discrete and multicell thunderstorms blossomed across this six-county North Central Kansas area on this Saturday afternoon, with a few storms briefly becoming severe and producing quarter size hail. Even though radar data suggested there were likely additional pockets of severe-criteria hail, the only limited ground-truth reports emanated from Zurich, from near Cedar and from rural northwestern Phillips County. Although the mesoscale environment was potentially supportive of a somewhat greater severe weather threat, the fairly rapid development of plentiful convection and resultant storm-scale outflow interference likely mitigated this. Despite the widespread activity, rainfall amounts across the vast majority of the local area averaged well-under 1. ||The first storms of the afternoon affected the local area between 3-4 p.m. CDT, as already-developed activity pushed into Mitchell County from the south and an isolated severe storm formed over southern Smith County. Over the course of the next few hours, several additional strong to marginally severe storms either popped up within, or pushed into the six-county area from the south, before convection steadily weakened and diminished in coverage between 6-7 p.m. CDT. In the mid-upper levels, the primary player was an expansive closed low churning slowly eastward through eastern Colorado, with several smaller-scale disturbances shifting northward into the Central Plains along its eastern flank. At the surface, there were no distinct synoptic features within North Central Kansas, only fairly weak, broad low pressure and prevailing light southerly to easterly breezes. Despite afternoon temperatures only rising into the low-70s F, the combination of surface dewpoints in the mid-50s and notably cold air aloft (evidenced by 500 millibar temps around -18 C) yielded mixed-layer CAPE values around 1000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.

Widespread wind, hail, and flooding occurred, starting overnight on the 24th into the 25th. While flooding was the biggest problem, mainly in Missouri for the morning of the 25th, severe weather moved in and brought a wide area of severe reports across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Olathe (OJC) recorded a 63 mph wind gust as storms moved through.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 6 foot diameter tree was uprooted as a result of strong winds that moved through the area.

A swath of damaging winds occurred across portions of north central Kansas early this Friday evening. Scattered thunderstorms developed over southwestern Nebraska during the afternoon hours. Between 4 and 5 p.m. CST, several storms combined into a very small bow echo configuration over Furnas County Nebraska. This storm entered northwestern Phillips County Kansas around 5 p.m. and subsequently raced east-southeast across north central Kansas, exiting Mitchell County by 730 p.m. CST. This cluster of storms was responsible for a swath of wind damage across Phillips, Smith and Mitchell counties. Multiple wind gusts were measured at 58 mph or above. The highest wind speed recorded was 73 mph at the Smith Center airport. Large tree limbs were also reported down in several locations. A separate isolated storm, tracking along the Nebraska-Kansas border, intensified over northeastern Jewell County. This storm also produced wind damage, overturning an irrigation pivot and scattering irrigation pipes.||These storms formed within a cold frontal zone. Aloft, a trough was over the Rockies with an embedded shortwave trough advancing through the Northern and Central Plains. A ridge was over the eastern United States. Temperatures in the upper 70s and dewpoints in the upper 50s combined with poor mid-level lapse rates to produce MLCAPE between 500 and 1000 J/kg. However, the dry sub-cloud layer did allow for storms to form along a common cold pool and accelerate. Deep layer shear was 35-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

Between 6-9 p.m. CDT on this late Sunday afternoon and evening, a fairly compact-but-intense supercell storm tracked eastward across mainly central sections of Smith and Jewell counties. This storm yielded several reports of large hail and also the first tornado of 2015 within this six-county North Central Kansas area, an EF-0 that touched down three miles north of Smith Center and tracked for about one mile, damaging the roof of an outbuilding. Prior to this brief tornado, there had been several reports of a funnel cloud or multiple funnels as the storm traversed west-central Smith County. For most of its path, this supercell was primarily a hail-producer, as quarter size stones fell in Lebanon, Burr Oak and Formoso, and larger stones up to golf ball size rained down on Mankato for several minutes. ||Focusing on event timing, this storm initiated over the Smith-Phillips County line around 6 p.m. CDT, just ahead of an approaching cold front. At first, it appeared storm mode might assume a southwest-northeast oriented line segment, but a more discrete supercell structure quickly evolved as it tracked east down the Highway 36 corridor. As this storm reached eastern Jewell County around 8 p.m. CDT, it began morphing into a more linear structure that expanded southwestward into Mitchell County for a time. However, there were no further severe weather reports as all thunderstorms exited the local area by 930 p.m. CDT, in tandem with the passing cold front. In the mid-upper levels, forcing was provided by the southern reaches of a broad shortwave trough tracking east across the Central and especially Northern Plains, with this trough ushering in increasing west-southwest flow aloft. At the surface, the well-defined, northeast-southwest oriented cold front steadily swept through North Central Kansas during the evening. Ahead of this boundary, afternoon temperatures across most of the local area topped out between 75-80 F as dewpoints rose into the mid-upper 50s on gusty south winds. The presence of a weak surface low and attendant warm front in the immediate area likely contributed to tornado development, as local surface observations revealed a backing of wind direction to southeasterly ahead of the storm. As for basic severe weather parameters, mixed-layer CAPE values peaked in the 1000-1500 J/kg range in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 40-50 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 rated tornado occurred north-northeast of Smith Center, KS. Damage was mainly limited to minor roof damage on an outbuilding, and the path was most likely intermittent. Maximum wind speed was estimated at 75 mph. A combination of radar data, storm survey, photos, and personal accounts were used to determine the approximate location and time of the tornado.

This cluster of storms was responsible for a swath of wind damage across Phillips, Smith and Mitchell counties. Multiple wind gusts were measured at 58 mph or above. The highest wind speed recorded was 73 mph at the Smith Center airport. Large tree limbs were also reported down in several locations. A separate isolated storm, tracking along the Nebraska-Kansas border, intensified over northeastern Jewell County. This storm also produced wind damage, overturning an irrigation pivot and scattering irrigation pipes.||These storms formed within a cold frontal zone. Aloft, a trough was over the Rockies with an embedded shortwave trough advancing through the Northern and Central Plains. A ridge was over the eastern United States. Temperatures in the upper 70s and dewpoints in the upper 50s combined with poor mid-level lapse rates to produce MLCAPE between 500 and 1000 J/kg. However, the dry sub-cloud layer did allow for storms to form along a common cold pool and accelerate. Deep layer shear was 35-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs between 6 and 8 inches in diameter were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous, semi-discrete and multicell thunderstorms blossomed across this six-county North Central Kansas area on this Saturday afternoon, with a few storms briefly becoming severe and producing quarter size hail. Even though radar data suggested there were likely additional pockets of severe-criteria hail, the only limited ground-truth reports emanated from Zurich, from near Cedar and from rural northwestern Phillips County. Although the mesoscale environment was potentially supportive of a somewhat greater severe weather threat, the fairly rapid development of plentiful convection and resultant storm-scale outflow interference likely mitigated this. Despite the widespread activity, rainfall amounts across the vast majority of the local area averaged well-under 1. ||The first storms of the afternoon affected the local area between 3-4 p.m. CDT, as already-developed activity pushed into Mitchell County from the south and an isolated severe storm formed over southern Smith County. Over the course of the next few hours, several additional strong to marginally severe storms either popped up within, or pushed into the six-county area from the south, before convection steadily weakened and diminished in coverage between 6-7 p.m. CDT. In the mid-upper levels, the primary player was an expansive closed low churning slowly eastward through eastern Colorado, with several smaller-scale disturbances shifting northward into the Central Plains along its eastern flank. At the surface, there were no distinct synoptic features within North Central Kansas, only fairly weak, broad low pressure and prevailing light southerly to easterly breezes. Despite afternoon temperatures only rising into the low-70s F, the combination of surface dewpoints in the mid-50s and notably cold air aloft (evidenced by 500 millibar temps around -18 C) yielded mixed-layer CAPE values around 1000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.

Widespread wind, hail, and flooding occurred, starting overnight on the 24th into the 25th. While flooding was the biggest problem, mainly in Missouri for the morning of the 25th, severe weather moved in and brought a wide area of severe reports across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a 70 mph wind gust in Olathe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave trough lifting out of the Rockies combined with an atmosphere that was recovering from earlier thunderstorms.  There likely was mesoscale interactions with old outflow boundaries.  Tornadoes formed along a warm front lifting north.  Many storm spotters and chasers were unable to see the tornadoes due to the proximately to dense fog on the cold side of the front.  Those that did observe the tornadoes were astonished at the apparent width and magnitude of the tornadoes.  However, it appears that the condensation part of the tornadoes were exceptionally large due to the cold and moist airmass being entrained into them.  They appeared to be much stronger than they actually were, likely because of the amount of condensation and associated very low cloud bases.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced hail to the size of nickels in far southern Osborne County (north of Luray) very early on this Friday morning. Several small thunderstorms initiated around midnight over Ellis County and quickly merged as they lifted northeast. Over the following two hours, other storms initiated over north central Kansas, forming a northwest-southeast oriented line. One storm embedded within the line was strong enough to produce this nickel size hail. ||A weak low pressure system was advancing through the Northern Plains. Low pressure was over Minnesota, with the associated cold front extending southwest across Nebraska into Wyoming. These storms formed in the warm sector, well ahead of the front. In the upper-levels, a longwave trough was over the western United States, with a ridge over the East. These storms developed just after the passage of a shortwave ridge. Synoptic forcing was negligible. While the strongest warm air advection was further north over Nebraska, these storms did initiate near the nose of a 30-40 knot low-level jet. MUCAPE was around 1000 J/kg and deep layer shear was around 20 knots.

A broken line of storms developed over northeastern Kansas and northwestern Missouri producing a few reports of large hail, winds and flooding. A bluff adjacent to Highway 7 at mile marker 246 collapsed obstructing one lane of the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed early this Wednesday afternoon and evening, and predominantly produced tornadoes and flash flooding. Five tornadoes occurred over Jewell County. With no cap in place, scattered thunderstorms began developing as early as the noon hour over north central Kansas. Most storms rapidly became supercellular. These supercells began producing severe weather by 330 p.m. CDT. It was about this time that a short line formed from the Jewell-Smith County line south into Osborne County. Supercells were embedded within this line and several discrete supercells developed ahead of it over Mitchell and Jewell counties. Eventually, an MCS developed. The leading edge of the cold pool becoming stationary, with new storms repeatedly developing over north central Kansas and further downstream into southeastern Nebraska. Cell training produced a swath of 4-8 inches of rain over portions of Jewell County. The highest amount reported was 7.92 inches near Webber. ||A complex low pressure system extended from the Canadian Prairies into the western United States. These storms developed in the warm sector, far away from any discernible surface boundaries. However, an axis of confluence was present over north central Kansas. A deep upper-level trough was over the western U.S., with a ridge over the East. In the southwest flow between these features, a shortwave trough was lifting through the Central Plains. These storms formed in the southeast quadrant of the associated vorticity maximum. Around the time thunderstorms developed, temperatures were in the lower 70s with dewpoints in the lower 60s, resulting in low cloud bases. Even at mid-afternoon, cloud bases were only 2000-2500 ft. The surface conditions and mid-level lapse rates of 7 degrees C/km resulted in SBCAPE was around 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was only 25-30 knots. However, low-level wind fields were strong with 40-45 knots at 850 mb. This resulted in large hodographs with 0-1 km SRH values of 200-250 m2/s2, and 0-3 km SRH of 250-300 m2/s2. Combined with low cloud bases, this created a favorable environment for supercells and tornadoes.

On the morning of the 15th a severe bow echo rolled through eastern KS causing wind damage. That evening a left over boundary allowed a supercell to form. This storm was long lived and produced large hail and severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous trees down in Jamestown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening law enforcement estimated wind gusts of 90 MPH from a severe thunderstorm near Tribune.  No damage was reported from these winds.  This same thunderstorm brought rapid, heavy rainfall to western parts of the county.  Law enforcement officers reported water over Highway 96 from Horace to Tribune.  Rainfall totals a mile or two west of Tribune ranged from 1.10 inches to 1.86 inches, with estimated rainfall amounts close to three inches between Horace and the county line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Highway 96 from Horace to Tribune.  With higher rainfall amounts to the west of this report area roads also had flooding in low lying areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms produced large hail over southeast Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A picture from social media showed baseball size hail near Highway 47 and Highway 3. Several residences had damaged roofs and windows to homes.  Several cars received severe damage with dents and cracked windshields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a fairly classic meteorological setup for June severe weather in place, numerous semi-discrete strong to severe thunderstorms blossomed across this six-county North Central Kansas area on this Wednesday evening the 3rd into early Thursday morning the 4th. While these storms yielded mainly quarter size hail, and one report up to 2 diameter south of Burr Oak, the storm of the night was a slow-moving tornadic supercell that peaked in intensity from far east-central Osborne into central Mitchell County between 10 p.m.-midnight CDT. This storm produced three confirmed after-dark tornadoes, two rated EF-1 with path lengths around one mile near the Osborne/Mitchell County line west of Tipton, and a brief EF-0 touchdown slightly east of Tipton. The two stronger tornadoes, one of which moved east and one west under the parent mesocyclone, damaged or destroyed several farm outbuildings, but no injuries occurred. Around midnight CDT, this storm began losing supercell structure and weakening as it drifted northeast through the Beloit area. In addition to tornadoes, this storm produced torrential rainfall of at least 3-4 in a short time across central and western portions of Mitchell County. Other storms produced a swath of heavy rain of generally 1.50-3.00 across much of southern Jewell County. However, the majority of the six-county area totaled no more than 0.50-1.00.  ||Breaking down the overall event timing, several strong to severe storms first blossomed over Rooks and Osborne counties between 700-830 p.m. CDT. Over the next few hours, activity gradually expanded to the north and east, as the aforementioned tornadic supercell took center stage around nightfall. Between 130-230 a.m. CDT on the 4th, storms vacated the entire six-county area to the north and east. Several hours later between 6-8 a.m. CDT, additional elevated storms flared up primarily east of Highway 281, but were non-severe. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale situation: with a generally west-east oriented surface front draped through the northern half of Kansas, seasonably strong instability and shear in place, and a strengthening 30-40 knot southerly low level jet ramping up during the night, this was a well-anticipated severe weather event. On the large scale, the Central Plains was under broad west-southwest flow aloft, well-downstream from a large trough centered over the West Coast. While modest capping held storms at bay through the afternoon, the combination of subtle, low-amplitude disturbances aloft and increasing lift along the developing low level jet sparked a rapid evening flare-up. At the surface, the evening hours featured steady easterly breezes and dewpoints in the mid-upper 60s F. Around the time the tornadic supercell developed, the mesoscale environment featured mixed-layer CAPE around 2000 J/kg, 0-6 kilometer deep-layer wind shear around 30 knots, and fairly strong 0-1 kilometer storm-relative helicity of at least 150 m2/s2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report was received via Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a fairly classic meteorological setup for June severe weather in place, numerous semi-discrete strong to severe thunderstorms blossomed across this six-county North Central Kansas area on this Wednesday evening the 3rd into early Thursday morning the 4th. While these storms yielded mainly quarter size hail, and one report up to 2 diameter south of Burr Oak, the storm of the night was a slow-moving tornadic supercell that peaked in intensity from far east-central Osborne into central Mitchell County between 10 p.m.-midnight CDT. This storm produced three confirmed after-dark tornadoes, two rated EF-1 with path lengths around one mile near the Osborne/Mitchell County line west of Tipton, and a brief EF-0 touchdown slightly east of Tipton. The two stronger tornadoes, one of which moved east and one west under the parent mesocyclone, damaged or destroyed several farm outbuildings, but no injuries occurred. Around midnight CDT, this storm began losing supercell structure and weakening as it drifted northeast through the Beloit area. In addition to tornadoes, this storm produced torrential rainfall of at least 3-4 in a short time across central and western portions of Mitchell County. Other storms produced a swath of heavy rain of generally 1.50-3.00 across much of southern Jewell County. However, the majority of the six-county area totaled no more than 0.50-1.00.  ||Breaking down the overall event timing, several strong to severe storms first blossomed over Rooks and Osborne counties between 700-830 p.m. CDT. Over the next few hours, activity gradually expanded to the north and east, as the aforementioned tornadic supercell took center stage around nightfall. Between 130-230 a.m. CDT on the 4th, storms vacated the entire six-county area to the north and east. Several hours later between 6-8 a.m. CDT, additional elevated storms flared up primarily east of Highway 281, but were non-severe. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale situation: with a generally west-east oriented surface front draped through the northern half of Kansas, seasonably strong instability and shear in place, and a strengthening 30-40 knot southerly low level jet ramping up during the night, this was a well-anticipated severe weather event. On the large scale, the Central Plains was under broad west-southwest flow aloft, well-downstream from a large trough centered over the West Coast. While modest capping held storms at bay through the afternoon, the combination of subtle, low-amplitude disturbances aloft and increasing lift along the developing low level jet sparked a rapid evening flare-up. At the surface, the evening hours featured steady easterly breezes and dewpoints in the mid-upper 60s F. Around the time the tornadic supercell developed, the mesoscale environment featured mixed-layer CAPE around 2000 J/kg, 0-6 kilometer deep-layer wind shear around 30 knots, and fairly strong 0-1 kilometer storm-relative helicity of at least 150 m2/s2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was widely scattered and mostly dime sized, but some hail was as large as quarters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado moved west, and began with damage to a silo and large metal building. On the tornado's trek, it tossed grain bin debris about three quarters of a mile. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms produced a small tornado that hit Eudora.  The tornado tracked around 6.5 miles doing sporadic tree damage along its path. Some portions of the line produced severe wind gusts and caused damage. Heavy rainfall caused flash flooding in Jackson and Shawnee counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated.

Several MCSs affected northeast Kansas during this 2-day event. Each round of storms brought hail, gusty winds, and flooding rains. Each of the MCSs formed near the NE/KS state line and moved southeastward across the area. The environment was able to recover after each round, and the convection remained partially surface based through out the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 10th a supercell formed west of Lyon county. It then moved into the county and began to bow out causing severe wind gusts in the process. Slow storm motion and heavy rainfall lead to flash flooding in Lyon and Osage counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of minor roof damage to a building.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding over the Kansas turnpike at mile marker 118. This shut down the northbound lanes. A 21 year old male driver hydroplaned and lost control of his vehicle. The vehicle left the road and was then swept downstream by the flood waters. He was not able to survive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave trough lifting out of the Rockies combined with an atmosphere that was recovering from earlier thunderstorms.  There likely was mesoscale interactions with old outflow boundaries.  Tornadoes formed along a warm front lifting north.  Many storm spotters and chasers were unable to see the tornadoes due to the proximately to dense fog on the cold side of the front.  Those that did observe the tornadoes were astonished at the apparent width and magnitude of the tornadoes.  However, it appears that the condensation part of the tornadoes were exceptionally large due to the cold and moist airmass being entrained into them.  They appeared to be much stronger than they actually were, likely because of the amount of condensation and associated very low cloud bases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado moved in from Meade County and turned north and then northwest dissipating just southeast of Montezuma.  A few pivot irrigation sprinklers were damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the hail was pea sized.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Beaver creek was out of its banks.  Received a total of 1.30 inches of rainfall.

A strong shortwave trough and associated 130 knot upper jet moved across western Kansas during the afternoon and evening.  At the surface frontogenesis along an secondary cold front ignited severe thunderstorms.  The thunderstorms moved rapidly and developed a very pronounced Rear Inflow Jet. As a consequence, the storms generated very high surface winds.   From Scott County the storms moved into Pratt county.  There were approximately 150 pivot irrigation sprinklers overturned or damaged along this stretch.  Total dollar amount for this entire area probably exceeded $7 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a fairly classic meteorological setup for June severe weather in place, numerous semi-discrete strong to severe thunderstorms blossomed across this six-county North Central Kansas area on this Wednesday evening the 3rd into early Thursday morning the 4th. While these storms yielded mainly quarter size hail, and one report up to 2 diameter south of Burr Oak, the storm of the night was a slow-moving tornadic supercell that peaked in intensity from far east-central Osborne into central Mitchell County between 10 p.m.-midnight CDT. This storm produced three confirmed after-dark tornadoes, two rated EF-1 with path lengths around one mile near the Osborne/Mitchell County line west of Tipton, and a brief EF-0 touchdown slightly east of Tipton. The two stronger tornadoes, one of which moved east and one west under the parent mesocyclone, damaged or destroyed several farm outbuildings, but no injuries occurred. Around midnight CDT, this storm began losing supercell structure and weakening as it drifted northeast through the Beloit area. In addition to tornadoes, this storm produced torrential rainfall of at least 3-4 in a short time across central and western portions of Mitchell County. Other storms produced a swath of heavy rain of generally 1.50-3.00 across much of southern Jewell County. However, the majority of the six-county area totaled no more than 0.50-1.00.  ||Breaking down the overall event timing, several strong to severe storms first blossomed over Rooks and Osborne counties between 700-830 p.m. CDT. Over the next few hours, activity gradually expanded to the north and east, as the aforementioned tornadic supercell took center stage around nightfall. Between 130-230 a.m. CDT on the 4th, storms vacated the entire six-county area to the north and east. Several hours later between 6-8 a.m. CDT, additional elevated storms flared up primarily east of Highway 281, but were non-severe. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale situation: with a generally west-east oriented surface front draped through the northern half of Kansas, seasonably strong instability and shear in place, and a strengthening 30-40 knot southerly low level jet ramping up during the night, this was a well-anticipated severe weather event. On the large scale, the Central Plains was under broad west-southwest flow aloft, well-downstream from a large trough centered over the West Coast. While modest capping held storms at bay through the afternoon, the combination of subtle, low-amplitude disturbances aloft and increasing lift along the developing low level jet sparked a rapid evening flare-up. At the surface, the evening hours featured steady easterly breezes and dewpoints in the mid-upper 60s F. Around the time the tornadic supercell developed, the mesoscale environment featured mixed-layer CAPE around 2000 J/kg, 0-6 kilometer deep-layer wind shear around 30 knots, and fairly strong 0-1 kilometer storm-relative helicity of at least 150 m2/s2.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado set down on the curve of the road about 3 miles west of Tipton, and moved southeast briefly before turning more east in direction, lifting about a mile farther. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although numerous thunderstorms rumbled across this six-county North Central Kansas area from the afternoon of Wednesday the 10th into the early morning of Thursday the 11th, the vast majority of them were sub-severe, producing only gusty winds and brief, heavy downpours. Storm mode primarily consisted of multicell clusters and lines, and the only ground-truth severe-criteria report was estimated 60 MPH gusts in northwestern Smith County at 9 p.m. CDT. During the preceding hour, this same small-scale linear complex yielded several reports of near-severe gusts in the 50-55 MPH range across Phillips County. As for rainfall, the majority of the local area only reported modest amounts of 0.50-1.00. However, much of Jewell and Mitchell counties tallied closer to 1.50, with a small section of southeast Jewell and northeast Mitchell, including the Randall area, getting drenched with at least 2.50-3.00. Despite a lack of ground-truth reports, it can be reasonably assumed that there were some short-term flooding issues in this area lasting into the day of the 11th. ||As for some timing details, the first few storms of the day initiated over Phillips and Smith counties between 5:45-7:00 p.m. CDT before quickly departing northward into Nebraska. Following a short lull, the next storm cluster marched into Phillips/Rooks counties from the west between 7:45-8:45 p.m. CDT and went on to produce the 60 MPH winds in Smith County. Through the remainder of the night, numerous non-severe storms continued streaming across the area, with most places finally rain-free by 6 a.m. CDT. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale setup: the primary trigger aloft was a series of fairly subtle disturbances gradually overspreading the Central Plains, out ahead of a modest shortwave trough drifting southeast toward the region from the Northern Rockies. At the surface, the most evident boundary near the local area during the afternoon was a trough axis extending southwestward to weak low pressure centered near the KS/CO border. With afternoon temperatures rising into the mid-upper 90s, storms developed in an environment featuring 1000-2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and around 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear. During the night, widespread weak convection persisted within the exit region of a 30-40 knot low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH resulted in a few tree limbs being downed and some minor shingle damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of 86th and Carlton rd is closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 10th a supercell formed west of Lyon county. It then moved into the county and began to bow out causing severe wind gusts in the process. Slow storm motion and heavy rainfall lead to flash flooding in Lyon and Osage counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of hwy 99 and 135th rd was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several MCSs affected northeast Kansas during this 2-day event. Each round of storms brought hail, gusty winds, and flooding rains. Each of the MCSs formed near the NE/KS state line and moved southeastward across the area. The environment was able to recover after each round, and the convection remained partially surface based through out the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water running over the road at 25th and Frontier rd, 26th and Jayhawk rd, and 23rd and Hackberry rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front advanced across the area during the afternoon and evening.  There were also residual outflow boundaries from convection the day before that provided a focusing area for thunderstorm development.

A cold front advanced across the area during the afternoon and evening.  There were also residual outflow boundaries from convection the day before that provided a focusing area for thunderstorm development.
Several long-track supercell thunderstorms developed during the evening hours and moved north. Seven tornadoes were reported over four hours, with some producing EF1-EF2 damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chaser reported utility poles were snapped just north of Highway 36 on Road 314.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave trough lifting out of the Rockies combined with an atmosphere that was recovering from earlier thunderstorms.  There likely was mesoscale interactions with old outflow boundaries.  Tornadoes formed along a warm front lifting north.  Many storm spotters and chasers were unable to see the tornadoes due to the proximately to dense fog on the cold side of the front.  Those that did observe the tornadoes were astonished at the apparent width and magnitude of the tornadoes.  However, it appears that the condensation part of the tornadoes were exceptionally large due to the cold and moist airmass being entrained into them.  They appeared to be much stronger than they actually were, likely because of the amount of condensation and associated very low cloud bases.

Numerous thunderstorms produced torrential rains across parts of South-Central Kansas for much of the afternoon and evening. Major flooding occurred. The most serious was that of a flash flood that occurred in Wichita. In parts of South and Southeast Wichita as well as West Wichita vehicles were submerged. Most of the parking lots at the Towne West Mall were flooded to depths around 3 feet. Rainfalls from 3-6 inches were widespread. Many streets were barricaded. Among them was Maple Avenue from I-235 to West Streets. The situation was exacerbated by heavy thunderstorms that produced torrential rains early in the morning. In fact, a staggering 5.60 inches was measured in Harper that morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KAKE Channel 10 reported the flooding at Towne West Mall parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very early in the morning of the 24th, an isolated thunderstorm produced an estimated 52 KT/60 MPH gust 5 miles northeast of Lincoln. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved over Northwest Kansas during the afternoon and evening.  The majority of the severe weather occurred during the afternoon in Logan county with several tornado reports in the Winona and Monument area.  The largest hail size reported was 1.75 inches near Winona, the same storm that produced the tornado.  Later on a line of storms over Thomas county produced severe wind gusts measuring 63 MPH at the Colby airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was intermittently in contact with the ground for about 10 minutes. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough and associated 130 knot upper jet moved across western Kansas during the afternoon and evening.  At the surface frontogenesis along an secondary cold front ignited severe thunderstorms.  The thunderstorms moved rapidly and developed a very pronounced Rear Inflow Jet. As a consequence, the storms generated very high surface winds.   From Scott County the storms moved into Pratt county.  There were approximately 150 pivot irrigation sprinklers overturned or damaged along this stretch.  Total dollar amount for this entire area probably exceeded $7 million.

Approximately 14 tornadoes were documented and confirmed via reports and radar data all occurring during the late afternoon and evening of May 6th.  The tornadoes impacted Republic, Cloud, Washington and Clay Counties.  In fact, 7 tornadoes alone occurred in Republic County.  This was the largest one day outbreak of tornadoes in the past 10 years for NWS Topeka.  The strongest tornado was an EF3 that destroyed a home south of Munden in Republic county.  The longest was over 25 miles in length also across Republic county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported an elephant trunk shaped tornado around 2  miles northwest of Washington. EM was not able to find any damage but area is open pasture so possible that touched down in pasture and did no damage.

Shortly after sunset on this Thursday evening, between 9:00-9:30 p.m. CDT, a southwest-to-northeast corridor of strong to marginally severe multicell storms ignited over the heart of this six-county North Central Kansas area. These slightly elevated storms primarily produced small hail and brief heavy rain before mostly-departing the local area to the south and east by 11 p.m. CDT. However, one storm pulsed up to severe thresholds over southern Mitchell County, yielding a quarter size hail report. Shortly after this first round of storms vacated the local area, yet another southwest-to-northeast complex of convection fired up over North Central Kansas between 11:00-11:30 p.m. CDT. Unlike its predecessor though, this round remained sub-severe as it too gradually departed to the south and east by around 2 a.m. CDT, ending all local thunderstorm activity for the night. Rainfall-wise, amounts varied rather widely, ranging from little to nothing (especially within northern portions of Phillips/Smith/Jewell counties) to pockets up to around 1 (especially within southern portions of Osborne/Jewell/Mitchell counties). Wettest of all was a portion of south central Mitchell County, which likely received at least 1.50-2.00. ||In terms of forcing mechanisms, the most pertinent feature was a modest evening increase in convergence along a fairly sharp, slightly-elevated frontal zone evident at 850 millibars. This boundary was stretched in west-to-east fashion directly over the local area, roughly 60-80 miles north of a corresponding surface front draped through central Kansas. As the night went on, this elevated frontal zone pressed southward, taking thunderstorm chances out of the local area with it. On the larger scale synoptic scene, lift was provided by a broad shortwave trough dropping southeastward into the region from the Northern Plains. Around the time of the quarter size hail report in Mitchell County, the mesoscale environment featured 1000-2000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE and around 40 knots of effective shear.

Several MCSs affected northeast Kansas during this 2-day event. Each round of storms brought hail, gusty winds, and flooding rains. Each of the MCSs formed near the NE/KS state line and moved southeastward across the area. The environment was able to recover after each round, and the convection remained partially surface based through out the event.

A strong shortwave trough and associated 130 knot upper jet moved across western Kansas during the afternoon and evening.  At the surface frontogenesis along an secondary cold front ignited severe thunderstorms.  The thunderstorms moved rapidly and developed a very pronounced Rear Inflow Jet. As a consequence, the storms generated very high surface winds.   From Scott County the storms moved into Pratt county.  There were approximately 150 pivot irrigation sprinklers overturned or damaged along this stretch.  Total dollar amount for this entire area probably exceeded $7 million.

One lone storm moved across portions of Central, KS and produced a limited amount of severe weather.
One lone storm moved across portions of Central, KS and produced a limited amount of severe weather.

Compared to the more concentrated zones of severe weather and/or flooding rains that plagued nearby areas to the north, west and east, this six-county portion of North Central Kansas fared quite well on the evening of Thursday the 4th into the early morning hours of Friday the 5th. In fact, the only local severe-criteria report consisted of quarter size hail west of Phillipsburg shortly after 9:30 p.m. CDT, produced by a briefly-severe, semi-discrete storm that drifted northeastward across the heart of the county. Otherwise, the vast majority of storms on this night produced nothing more than sub-severe wind gusts and pockets of moderate to briefly-heavy rainfall. Based on a combination of gauge reports and radar estimation, the majority of the six-county area averaged well-under 1 of rain, except for higher totals potentially in excess of 1.50 that fell primarily within northeastern Jewell, northern Phillips and extreme northwestern Rooks counties. Even so, there were no known reports of impactful flooding issues. ||Taking a closer look at the timing and evolution of local thunderstorm activity, the first few semi-discrete storms developed into Phillips and northern Rooks counties between 8:30-10:00 p.m. CDT, including the one that produced quarter size hail. Through the remainder of the night and lasting into morning hours on the 5th, numerous, primarily weak storms affected much of the local area. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale situation: this was a fairly similar setup to the one that produced the more significant severe weather event within North Central Kansas the night before, as a quasi-stationary, composite surface front/outflow boundary remained in place near the Nebraska-Kansas border. However, this time around no organized supercell storms were able to develop, and the most vigorous late-night convection focused slightly north and east of the local area within the primary exit region of a 30-40 knot southerly low level jet. On the large scale, the parent mid-upper trough remained well to the west along the California coast as a series of downstream low-amplitude disturbances passed through the Central Plains in quasi-zonal flow, interacting with a rather unstable airmass to trigger vigorous convection. Around the time of the Phillips County hail report, the mesoscale environment featured 2000-3000 J/kg mixed layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep-layer wind shear.

A strong short wave trough lifting out of the Rockies combined with an atmosphere that was recovering from earlier thunderstorms.  There likely was mesoscale interactions with old outflow boundaries.  Tornadoes formed along a warm front lifting north.  Many storm spotters and chasers were unable to see the tornadoes due to the proximately to dense fog on the cold side of the front.  Those that did observe the tornadoes were astonished at the apparent width and magnitude of the tornadoes.  However, it appears that the condensation part of the tornadoes were exceptionally large due to the cold and moist airmass being entrained into them.  They appeared to be much stronger than they actually were, likely because of the amount of condensation and associated very low cloud bases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a brief tornado under the mesocyclone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong to severe storms moved across northeastern Kansas and northwestern Missouri during the evening of May 4th. The storms produced localized flash flooding as well as some 1 hail reports.

During the late afternoon and into the evening a line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved out of Colorado into Northwest Kansas.  The largest hail size reported was golf ball size in Sharon Springs.  Winds from the thunderstorms blew down large elm trees near Weskan, uprooting two of them due to estimated speeds of 100 MPH.  The high winds also caused blowing dust, dropping visibilities to less than a quarter mile near Monument.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large elm trees were blown down, including one of the trees being uprooted.  A larger tree by the creek, with a diameter of 2.5 to 3 feet, was also uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening law enforcement estimated wind gusts of 90 MPH from a severe thunderstorm near Tribune.  No damage was reported from these winds.  This same thunderstorm brought rapid, heavy rainfall to western parts of the county.  Law enforcement officers reported water over Highway 96 from Horace to Tribune.  Rainfall totals a mile or two west of Tribune ranged from 1.10 inches to 1.86 inches, with estimated rainfall amounts close to three inches between Horace and the county line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 90 MPH wind gust was reported with the severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough and associated 130 knot upper jet moved across western Kansas during the afternoon and evening.  At the surface frontogenesis along an secondary cold front ignited severe thunderstorms.  The thunderstorms moved rapidly and developed a very pronounced Rear Inflow Jet. As a consequence, the storms generated very high surface winds.   From Scott County the storms moved into Pratt county.  There were approximately 150 pivot irrigation sprinklers overturned or damaged along this stretch.  Total dollar amount for this entire area probably exceeded $7 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 100 foot building was completely destroyed.

High terrain thunderstorms moved out of Colorado associated with a passing short wave trough and helped trigger thunderstorms, some severe.  One storm in particular developed and became supercellular and turned south.  It caused extensive damage along it's path shredding corn fields to nubs and killing numerous wildlife.  Thirty-three pivot sprinklers were overturned.  Several high voltage transmission line structures were crumpled by the high wind with speeds in excess of 110 MPH likely.||There was also reports of corn fields (6 foot high stalks) that were completely shredded with nothing but nubs left.  Wildlife in these fields were killed with numerous reports of dead deer, rapids and other critters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS in northwest flow aloft moved out across the plains.  Early on though widely scattered thunderstorms in a very unstable airmass were able to produce severe weather, including a landspout tornado that was highly visible and which moved across a lake.

A strong short wave trough lifting out of the Rockies combined with an atmosphere that was recovering from earlier thunderstorms.  There likely was mesoscale interactions with old outflow boundaries.  Tornadoes formed along a warm front lifting north.  Many storm spotters and chasers were unable to see the tornadoes due to the proximately to dense fog on the cold side of the front.  Those that did observe the tornadoes were astonished at the apparent width and magnitude of the tornadoes.  However, it appears that the condensation part of the tornadoes were exceptionally large due to the cold and moist airmass being entrained into them.  They appeared to be much stronger than they actually were, likely because of the amount of condensation and associated very low cloud bases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado damaged high voltage transmission lines and poles, pivot irrigation sprinklers, a home and trees.  A stock trailer was tossed through the side of the home but fortunately all occupants were in the basement.  A car in the driveway was carried and then dropped in a field about 100 miles to the north-northeast.  There were no roll marks from the vehicle.  The tornado moved into Ford County.

With a fairly classic meteorological setup for June severe weather in place, numerous semi-discrete strong to severe thunderstorms blossomed across this six-county North Central Kansas area on this Wednesday evening the 3rd into early Thursday morning the 4th. While these storms yielded mainly quarter size hail, and one report up to 2 diameter south of Burr Oak, the storm of the night was a slow-moving tornadic supercell that peaked in intensity from far east-central Osborne into central Mitchell County between 10 p.m.-midnight CDT. This storm produced three confirmed after-dark tornadoes, two rated EF-1 with path lengths around one mile near the Osborne/Mitchell County line west of Tipton, and a brief EF-0 touchdown slightly east of Tipton. The two stronger tornadoes, one of which moved east and one west under the parent mesocyclone, damaged or destroyed several farm outbuildings, but no injuries occurred. Around midnight CDT, this storm began losing supercell structure and weakening as it drifted northeast through the Beloit area. In addition to tornadoes, this storm produced torrential rainfall of at least 3-4 in a short time across central and western portions of Mitchell County. Other storms produced a swath of heavy rain of generally 1.50-3.00 across much of southern Jewell County. However, the majority of the six-county area totaled no more than 0.50-1.00.  ||Breaking down the overall event timing, several strong to severe storms first blossomed over Rooks and Osborne counties between 700-830 p.m. CDT. Over the next few hours, activity gradually expanded to the north and east, as the aforementioned tornadic supercell took center stage around nightfall. Between 130-230 a.m. CDT on the 4th, storms vacated the entire six-county area to the north and east. Several hours later between 6-8 a.m. CDT, additional elevated storms flared up primarily east of Highway 281, but were non-severe. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale situation: with a generally west-east oriented surface front draped through the northern half of Kansas, seasonably strong instability and shear in place, and a strengthening 30-40 knot southerly low level jet ramping up during the night, this was a well-anticipated severe weather event. On the large scale, the Central Plains was under broad west-southwest flow aloft, well-downstream from a large trough centered over the West Coast. While modest capping held storms at bay through the afternoon, the combination of subtle, low-amplitude disturbances aloft and increasing lift along the developing low level jet sparked a rapid evening flare-up. At the surface, the evening hours featured steady easterly breezes and dewpoints in the mid-upper 60s F. Around the time the tornadic supercell developed, the mesoscale environment featured mixed-layer CAPE around 2000 J/kg, 0-6 kilometer deep-layer wind shear around 30 knots, and fairly strong 0-1 kilometer storm-relative helicity of at least 150 m2/s2.

A stationary boundary was draped across Central Kansas during the overnight and early morning hours of June 5th, 2015.  Showers and thunderstorms developed along this front (generally along Interstate 70), and trained along this boundary.   As the ealry morning progressed and complex of storms moved out of Northwest Kansas and moved along this boundary as well, to produce torrential rainfall across Central Kansas.  So reports of flash flooding occurred, due to rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of ninth and Republic had water over the road. Several other streets were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High terrain thunderstorms moved out of Colorado associated with a passing short wave trough and helped trigger thunderstorms, some severe.  One storm in particular developed and became supercellular and turned south.  It caused extensive damage along it's path shredding corn fields to nubs and killing numerous wildlife.  Thirty-three pivot sprinklers were overturned.  Several high voltage transmission line structures were crumpled by the high wind with speeds in excess of 110 MPH likely.||There was also reports of corn fields (6 foot high stalks) that were completely shredded with nothing but nubs left.  Wildlife in these fields were killed with numerous reports of dead deer, rapids and other critters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported to be blown down at the intersection of Roads J and 10.

An intense thunderstorm developed over Cheyenne County in eastern Colorado and collapsed as it crossed into Kansas.  Winds estimated at 100 mph produced significant damage to a residence in far northwestern Greeley county as well as to a dairy farming operation further east. A dust storm accompanied the intense burst of winds with a wall of dirt and visibility to near zero reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense downburst swept across northern Greeley County producing significant damage and a dust storm dropping visibility to near zero in blowing dirt.  Damage included overturned irrigation pivots, downed utility poles, sides and roofs of outbuilding removed and damage to a dairy farming operation.  Gravel was scoured off county roads and embedded in trees and utility poles.  Damage was consistent with winds of approximately 100 mph.  The strong outflow continued across northern Wichita County and ultimately into Scott county.

Several clusters of strong to severe storms developed during the afternoon and evening over Northwest Kansas, some of them remaining stationary at times.  The largest hail size reported was quarter size near Blakeman, however over three inches of small hail was reported near Edson.  The strongest wind gust reported was an estimated 70 MPH at Winona, which occurred when the town to lose power.  A measured wind speed of 65 MPH was reported near Leoti ahead of a line of storms.  Flash flooding occurred near Winona with these storms as water 18 inches deep and roughly 100 yards wide was reported over Road 230.
After midnight a severe thunderstorm developed south of Sharon Springs.  Large hail up to quarter size was reported.
A surface trough moved into an unstable atmosphere producing scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to 10 power poles were blown down by the wind.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small tree limbs down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While conditions were not particularly hot, they were slightly above average for the mid to late summer. A 15 year old boy was out riding his bike near Osawatomie when he complained to his friend of being too hot, so he turned around to go home. He was found near train tracks near 335th Street and Bethel Church Road about 5 p.m. August 3. His autopsy determined dehydration being a significant factor in his death.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong November severe weather episode occurred across the region.  A very strong upper level low approached with with strong winds aloft.  An unusually high amount of moisture and unseasonably unstable airmass were transported north into Kansas. Large, long tracked and rapidly moving tornadoes occurred along with instances of large and damaging hail.

A strong upper level low swept through the Southern Rockies and into the Central Plains November 16th and 17th before exiting the area on the 18th. Wrap around moisture from this low and frontogenesis formed across northwestern Kansas and eastern Colorado early in the morning causing snow to fall and persisted throughout the day. This band of snow then slowly shifted eastward during the evening and overnight hours. Over 6 inches of snow fell outside of our forecast area with lesser amounts in the counties mentioned above.  Snowfall greater than 2 inches fell in portions northwestern Scott, northern Kearney, a majority of Hamilton, western Stanton, and western Morton counties. The highest reported snowfall was 6 inches located 11 miles north of Coolidge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches was observed at a location about 8 miles north of Coolidge.

Several clusters of strong to severe storms developed during the afternoon and evening over Northwest Kansas, some of them remaining stationary at times.  The largest hail size reported was quarter size near Blakeman, however over three inches of small hail was reported near Edson.  The strongest wind gust reported was an estimated 70 MPH at Winona, which occurred when the town to lose power.  A measured wind speed of 65 MPH was reported near Leoti ahead of a line of storms. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of light snow began during the very early morning hours before sunrise Saturday, January 31st, primarily along and east of Highway 281. The temperature profile was very marginal, but snow continued to fall through the day. It did mix with rain at times. Due to its persistence, some wet accumulations of 1 to 2 inches occurred on grassy surfaces. During the evening hours, snow became widespread as an organized snow band formed in the deformation zone of an intensifying low pressure center. Snow fell over nearly all of north central Kansas for a time, but the heaviest and most persistent snow fell where it had been snowing during the daylight hours of Saturday, east of Highway 281. By 4 am CST Sunday, the accumulating snow ended and exited into northeast Kansas. However, some smaller bands of light snow lingered through mid-morning. Storm total snowfall was generally 2 to 5 inches from eastern Smith into Jewell counties. The highest amount of 5.8 inches was measured at Mankato. As the snow ended, north winds dramatically increased as the low pressure system organized. Winds were sustained around 30 mph with gusts up to 45 mph. The cooling of the temperature profile allowed for the snow character to become drier after sunset. The result was significant blowing and drifting that lasted through the afternoon hours. The visibility was below one mile at times in blowing snow.||The initial wet snow Saturday occurred in warm air advection, in the entrance region of a 150 kt upper-level jet streak. The forcing for precipitation changed, however, as an arctic cold front sagged into Nebraska with lee cyclogenesis occurring ahead of it over southeastern Colorado. As the front continued moving south into Kansas Saturday night, the weak low left Colorado and progressed east along the front. It was located near Kansas City by daybreak Sunday and advanced east into Indiana by sunset. It was this low that was responsible for the majority of the snowfall accumulation which occurred Saturday night. The central pressure never lowered to less than 1005 mb and there was no deepening. However, the pressure gradient was significant northwest of the low, resulting in the strong winds. As with much of this winter, the flow aloft was split with a ridge along the West Coast, a closed low over the Desert Southwest, and confluent flow into the trough over the eastern United States. This low formed as a result of a shortwave trough that came out of western Canada and amplified as it approached the longwave trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low swept through the Southern Rockies and into the Central Plains November 16th and 17th before exiting the area on the 18th. Wrap around moisture from this low and frontogenesis formed across northwestern Kansas and eastern Colorado early in the morning causing snow to fall and persisted throughout the day. This band of snow then slowly shifted eastward during the evening and overnight hours. Over 6 inches of snow fell outside of our forecast area with lesser amounts in the counties mentioned above.  Snowfall greater than 2 inches fell in portions northwestern Scott, northern Kearney, a majority of Hamilton, western Stanton, and western Morton counties. The highest reported snowfall was 6 inches located 11 miles north of Coolidge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was generally around 4 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern set up over the Central Plains and Middle to Lower Missouri Valley. Rain with embedded thunderstorms occurred the night of the 23rd into the 24th with some of the storms producing small hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts around 45 MPH and falling snow caused visibilities to fall to zero at times over parts of Northwest Kansas during the afternoon and into the evening.  The reduced visibilities and icy roads caused I-70 to be closed across portions of Northwest Kansas, as well as Highway 24 in Sherman county, for around 7 1/2 hours.  Lodging quickly filled up causing a need for local shelters in Goodland to be opened.  Highways were reopened during the late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of visibilities near or at zero during the afternoon and evening.  Highway 24 was closed across the county and I-70 was closed from the state line to Goodland.

Lodging quickly filled up causing a need for local shelters in Goodland to be opened.  Highways were reopened during the late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: I-70 closed from Sherman county line to atleast Colby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe storms occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 8th, 2015. Storms first developed over Southern Kansas and quickly raced into Southeast Kansas during the afternoon. These storms produced 70 to 80 mph winds along with hail up to the size of golf balls. A second round of supercell thunderstorms eventually fired back to the west along a retreating dry line during the early evening over South Central Kansas and Northern Oklahoma. These storms produced several reports of  very large hail and eventually tracked into Kingman county where hail over 3 inches was reported which is larger than the size of baseballs. A brief tornado touchdown occurred southeast of Zenda, Kansas. ||Another supercell thunderstorm formed over eastern Kingman county. Rotating wall clouds were reported near the town of Cheney and a tornado touched down in extreme northwest Sedgwick County and tracked into Southeast Reno county.  The tornado was rated an EF0.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down across the county. Several roads were blocked due to downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong tornado moved southwest from Rice into Reno county causing significant damage to two different homesteads.  One home was completely demolished while another took a glancing blow.  Numerous trees were uprooted and those that were not had a significant amount of debarking.  Several other storms moved across the area producing severe wind and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rope tornado was reported as it moved unconventionally to the southwest.  One home took minor damage right after touchdown.  A few windows were blown out and a man was blown out of his chair.  Otherwise, significant tree damage (DI27 DOD3) was noted along the 150 yard wide path. This tornado moved into Reno County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet snow began to fall late Saturday Jan 31 across northern Kansas and continued into Sunday Feb 1. Much of the impacted area received anywhere from 4 to 8 inches of snow with a few higher amounts reported. Much colder air arrived during the morning of Feb 1 which along with gusty winds produced widespread blowing and drifting of the snow. Impacts included closed roads and schools in some spots were closed on Feb 2 due to the blowing and drifting of snow across roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer in Hiawatha measured 6 inches of snow.  Some sleet was also reported during the event.  Northwest winds of 20 to 30 mph developed during the day Sunday Feb 1 which caused blowing and drifting of snow and closed some county roads. Some schools were also closed Monday Feb 2 due to the blowing and drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave surged southeast from the Northern Rockies to the Central & Southern Plains. This promoted surface cyclogenesis over the Western Plains that in turn caused a stationary front that was situated along the Red River to undergo warm frontogenesis with the front reaching the Oklahoma/Kansas border late that afternoon. The transition enabled rich Gulf moisture to likewise spread to the Oklahoma/Kansas border. All combined to trigger scattered severe thunderstorms across South-Central & Southeast Kansas for most of the afternoon.

An upper level low spun around the Southwest United States during this time frame with weak upper level shortwaves being ejected eastward across the Central Rockies into the Central High Plains. This caused diffluent westerly flow above the Central High Plains. A strong upper level jet was also in place above the area of concern. Mid-level Isentropic lift of saturated air was present during this event and was the main lifting mechanism. A dome of high pressure shifted eastward as the weekend approached allowing southeast upslope winds to be felt across western Kansas. Light to moderate rain started to fall across far western Kansas by late Saturday morning, spreading eastward as the afternoon progressed. Precipitation then changed over to snow by evening, first across west central Kansas, then the remainder of southwest Kansas by midnight. Moderate snow showers were observed throughout the night with occasional light snow reported throughout Sunday. Light to moderate snow fell once again across portions of western Kansas Sunday night before ending by late Monday morning. By the time the snow ended, snowfall amounts of 6-10 inches were reported across west central Kansas, 3-7 inches across southwest Kansas, and 1-4 inches across central and south central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of April 8, several severe thunderstorms rolled through the area, bringing with it mostly large hail. These storms even brought 1.5 inch hail to the NWS office in Pleasant Hill, Missouri. On the Kansas side, the only hail reports were from the morning convection, but in Missouri the second round in the afternoon and evening brought a long swatch of wind that went into Central Missouri. In the Kansas City metro area a left-moving short-lived storm brought 2 inch hail that afternoon to the northern part of the metro.

A modest dryline developed  across South Central and Central Kansas during the afternoon hours of May 6th, 2015.  Moderate instability and shear combined to produce supercell thunderstorms across South Central Kansas and Central Kansas. |The supercell thunderstorms produced some large hail, but also produced five  tornadoes across the area.  One of the tornadoes was rated an EF3, with another rated an EF2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down in an open field and quickly grew in strength as it moved slightly east of north.  The tornado damaged 3 center pivot irrigation systems and caused damage at a farmstead by demolishing a single car detached garage and ripped off a carport that was attached to a barn.  As the tornado moved on to the north, it encountered another farmstead.  One house was directly in the path of the tornado and was completely leveled with nothing left standing (DI2 DOD9).  The home was built in the late 1800's and was not secured to the foundation.  Another home just to the northwest of the main home sustained significant damage to the southeast corner by taking down two small sections of the home.  Another barn on the property was a complete loss and the trees experienced significant limb loss and some debarkation. One minor injury was reported.  The person affected refused treatment. The tornado was 125 yards wide. This tornado moved into Harvey County.

A quasi stationary boundary was located over Southern Kansas during the afternoon of April 2nd, 2015.  A very unstable airmass was located just to the south of this boundary over extreme Southeast Kansas. Severe thunderstorms developed rapidly over Southeast Kansas and became supercellular during the evening hours. The supercell thunderstorms  produced two brief tornadoes in Labette County.  One tornado produced minor damage to a shed, while the other touched down in an open field.
